comment_text.sn7.20:1.4: “Almsman” (bhikkhaka) is almost identical with the standard word for a Buddhist mendicant (bhikkhu), but typically it is used of  almsgoing brahmins (eg. Manu 3.243).
comment_text.sn7.20:2.3: The Sanskrit parallel at Uv 32:18 read veśmāṁ dharmaṁ, confirming that vissa here means “domestic”.

root_text.sn7.20:1.2: Atha kho bhikkhako brāhmaṇo yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavatā saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.sn7.20:1.3: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho bhikkhako brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn7.20:1.4: “ahampi kho, bho gotama, bhikkhako, bhavampi bhikkhako, idha no kiṁ nānākaraṇan”ti?
root_text.sn7.20:4.1: Evaṁ vutte, bhikkhako brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn7.20:4.3: upāsakaṁ maṁ bhavaṁ gotamo dhāretu ajjatagge pāṇupetaṁ saraṇaṁ gatan”ti.

translation_text.sn7.20:1.2: Then a brahmin almsman went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.sn7.20:1.3: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn7.20:1.4: “Worthy Gotama, both you and I are almsmen. What, then, is the difference between us?”
translation_text.sn7.20:2.2: just by begging alms from others.
translation_text.sn7.20:2.3: One who has undertaken domestic duties
translation_text.sn7.20:2.4: has not yet become a mendicant.
translation_text.sn7.20:3.1: But one living a spiritual life
translation_text.sn7.20:3.2: who has banished both merit and evil,
translation_text.sn7.20:3.3: and wanders having appraised the world,
translation_text.sn7.20:3.4: is said to be a mendicant.”
translation_text.sn7.20:4.1: When he had spoken, the brahmin almsman said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn7.20:4.3: From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.”
